title: Blindsight
author: Peter Watts
description: A first-contact novel that uses aliens as a scalpel on the question of consciousness.
note: The most unsettling argument against consciousness ever written. Watts asks whether awareness is an evolutionary dead end.

title: The Three-Body Problem
author: Liu Cixin
description: A civilisation-scale first-contact story that uses physics as its moral framework.
note: Scale as a literary device. The universe as a dark forest. Changed how I think about civilisational risk.

title: Thinking in Systems
author: Donella Meadows
description: The clearest introduction to systems thinking available. A lens you can't unsee.
note: The clearest introduction to systems thinking I've encountered. Every page has a sentence worth underlining.

title: The Righteous Mind
author: Jonathan Haidt
description: Why smart people hold incompatible moral frameworks — and what to do about it.
note: Essential for anyone who works on change management. Why smart people hold incompatible moral frameworks.

title: Multipliers: How the Best Leaders Make Everyone Smarter
author: Liz Wiseman
description: The smartest person in the room is often its biggest bottleneck — not through malice, but through the accumulated habit of believing their contribution is indispensable.
note: Read this after a project where I was the bottleneck without knowing it. The 'Accidental Diminisher' chapter named something I'd been doing for two years.

title: Good Power
author: Ginni Rometty
description: The four-year degree was never a proxy for ability — it was a proxy for cheap filtering, and the skills gap it created is the compounding cost of that convenience.
note: The Boundary Paradox section is worth the whole book. The claim that setting limits increases your perceived value rather than reducing it took me a while to fully believe, then I watched it work.
